researchFindings.claim: Bacteria grow most rapidly between 40°F and 140°F (4°C and 60°C), known as the 'Danger Zone,' and can double in number in as little as 20 minutes in this range.
researchFindings.sourceOrganization: USDA Food Safety and Inspection Service
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Core background concept for understanding why temperature control matters; summary and quickAnswer material

researchFindings.claim: An unopened refrigerator will keep food safe for up to 4 hours during a power outage. A full freezer holds safe temperature for approximately 48 hours (24 hours if half full) with the door closed.
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Food and Water Safety During Power Outages and Floods
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Key time windows; whenToUse context and core reference content

researchFindings.claim: Discard refrigerated perishable food (meat, poultry, fish, eggs, milk, cut fruits and vegetables, and leftovers) after 4 hours without power or a cold source.
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Keep Food Safe After a Disaster or Emergency
researchFindings.sourceOrganization: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Discard rules for perishable foods; warnings and whatNotToDo

researchFindings.claim: Hard cheeses, butter, unopened condiments (ketchup, mustard, pickles), jelly, peanut butter, whole uncut fruits and vegetables, bread, and vinegar-based dressings remain safe after a power outage.
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Safe-to-keep food categories; core reference content

researchFindings.claim: Food may be safely refrozen if it still contains ice crystals or is at 40°F or below, though quality may suffer.
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Keep Your Food Safe During Emergencies: Power Outages, Floods & Fires
researchFindings.sourceOrganization: USDA Food Safety and Inspection Service
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Refreeze decision rule; core reference content

researchFindings.claim: Never taste food to determine if it is safe. You cannot rely on appearance or odor alone to determine food safety. When in doubt, throw it out.
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Food and Water Safety During Power Outages and Floods

researchFindings.claim: Transfer refrigerated perishable food to a cooler with ice or frozen gel packs if power will be out for more than 4 hours. Keep temperature at or below 40°F.
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Cooler use guidance; preferredAction and backupAction

researchFindings.claim: Fifty pounds of dry ice should hold an 18-cubic-foot fully stocked freezer cold for two days. Do not touch dry ice with bare hands or place it in direct contact with food.
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Keep Your Food Safe During Emergencies: Power Outages, Floods & Fires
researchFindings.sourceOrganization: USDA Food Safety and Inspection Service
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Dry ice guidance; reference content and warnings

researchFindings.claim: If perishable food has been held at room temperature for 2 hours or more (1 hour if temperature is above 90°F), it should be discarded.
researchFindings.sourceOrganization: USDA Food Safety and Inspection Service

researchFindings.claim: Stock canned foods, dry mixes, and other staples that do not require refrigeration, cooking, water, or special preparation. Include a manual can opener and eating utensils.
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Shelf-stable food guidance; preparednessTips and reference content

researchFindings.claim: Keep appliance thermometers in both the refrigerator and freezer to monitor temperatures. The refrigerator should be at 40°F or below and the freezer at 0°F or below.
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Food and Water Safety During Power Outages and Floods

researchFindings.claim: Freeze containers of water and gel packs to help keep food cold. Freeze refrigerated items you may not need immediately (milk, meat, leftovers) to extend safe storage time.
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Food and Water Safety During Power Outages and Floods

researchFindings.claim: Heat destroys active bacteria but not toxins already produced during temperature abuse. Staphylococcus aureus creates heat-stable toxins that cause illness even after thorough cooking.
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Keep Your Food Safe During Emergencies: Power Outages, Floods & Fires
researchFindings.sourceOrganization: USDA Food Safety and Inspection Service
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Why cooking temperature-abused food is unsafe; warnings and whatNotToDo

researchFindings.claim: Do not place perishable food outside in the snow during winter storms. Outside temperatures vary and food can be exposed to unsanitary conditions and animals.
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Keep Your Food Safe During Emergencies: Power Outages, Floods & Fires
researchFindings.sourceOrganization: USDA Food Safety and Inspection Service

researchFindings.claim: Perishable foods with temperatures at 45°F or below (measured with a food thermometer) should be safe but should be cooked and consumed as soon as possible.
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Food and Water Safety During Power Outages and Floods

researchFindings.claim: Plan to have at least a 3-day supply of non-perishable food on hand for emergencies. Store at least 1 gallon of water per person per day.
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Keep Food Safe After a Disaster or Emergency
researchFindings.sourceOrganization: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

researchFindings.claim: Commercially canned food may be eaten out of the can without warming. Alternative cooking sources in emergencies include candle warmers, chafing dishes, fondue pots, or a fireplace. Charcoal grills and camp stoves are for outdoor use only.
Throw out cans or food containers that spurt liquid or foam when opened, or contain food that is discolored, moldy, or smells bad.
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Keep Food Safe After a Disaster or Emergency
researchFindings.sourceOrganization: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Signs of unsafe canned food; redFlags

identifiedGaps.area: Specific guidance on using snow or outdoor cold as food storage
identifiedGaps.impact: USDA says not to use snow; no authoritative source provides safe protocols for using outdoor cold as a makeshift refrigerator. The guidance is simply 'don't do it.'
identifiedGaps.suggestion: Include the USDA warning against outdoor food storage in whatNotToDo

identifiedGaps.area: Quantified dry ice amounts for smaller freezers
identifiedGaps.impact: USDA provides 50-lb/18-cu-ft benchmark but no scaled guidance for smaller units
identifiedGaps.suggestion: Include the 50-lb benchmark and note users should scale accordingly

recommendation: The Writing skill should produce a comprehensive reference_guide covering: the danger zone concept, time windows for refrigerator/freezer safety, keep-vs-discard decision rules, cooler and dry ice strategies, shelf-stable alternatives, and the critical 'when in doubt throw it out' principle. The guide upgrades the stub food-preservation guide and should focus squarely on emergency power-outage food safety, not long-term homesteading preservation. All 18 research findings are from authoritative government sources (USDA, FDA, CDC, FEMA/Ready.gov, Red Cross), supporting a 'strong' source quality rating.
